details.text: Derivative: رَقِيبًا (Raqeeb) is the One who closely watches and observes and also the One who preserves. It implies that Allah is constantly watching and constantly preserving (or recording) what you do. رَقَبَة (Raqabah) refers to “a neck” which indicates to the closeness of Allah’s watching over His slaves.
Derivative: مُرَاغَمًا comes from the word رِغَام (Righaam) which is dirt or soil. Here it refers to land that someone can emigrate to.

Derivative: اطْمَأْنَنتُمْ is from اِطْمَأَنَّ – اِطِمِئْنان which means to be tranquil, at ease, secure and assured. Here it means to be in a state where you no longer feel any fear from being attacked by the enemy.

Derivative: خَوَّانًا (Khawwaan) is the hyperbolised form of خَائِن (Khaa’in) who is the one who has cheated others and betrayed their trust. خَوَّانًا (Khawwaan) is the one who repeatedly engages in that disloyal and treacherous conduct.

Derivative: أَثِيمًا (Atheem) is the hyperbolised form of آثِم (Aathim) which means “the sinful person” i.e., someone who has undertaken a sinful act. أَثِيمًا (Atheem) refers to the one whose sinfulness continues and keeps going.

Derivative: تَدْرُونَ is from دَرَى – يَدْرِي – دِرَايَة and means to know or be aware of something. In Arabic it is usually only used in the negative like لَا أَدْرِي “I don’t know” and implies a complete lack of knowledge like the expression “I don’t have a clue” or “I really have no idea”.

- “And whoever (وَمَن) earns (or commits) (يَكْسِبْ) a Khatee’ah (خَطِيئَةً) or (أَوْ) an Ithm (إِثْمًا)” – Both Khatee’ah (خَطِيئَةً) and Ithm (إِثْمًا) refer to a sinful act and there is some disagreement regarding there difference. Some have said that the former refers to a smaller sin whilst the latter a bigger sin. Others have said that the former relates to a sin that is undertaken unintentionally and the latter intentionally. It has also been said that the former is unintentional and intentional whilst the latter is only intentional. And it has been said that Khatee’ah (خَطِيئَةً) refers to the type of sin that someone does without caring or being concerned about it.
Derivative: مَرِيدًا (Mareed) is someone who goes out of his way to be rebellious and is insolent in that.
Derivative: أُمَنِّيَ is from مَنَّى – يُمَنِّي – تَمْنِيَة which means “to inspire, raise or fill with hopes and aspirations”.
Derivative: كَلَالَةً refers to a person who has no parents or children to receive inheritance from him.

Derivative: مَحِيصًا comes from مَحَصَ which means to extract and is used in the context of extracting impurities or filth from something to make it clear or to purify it. مَحِيصًا refers here to a place to be extracted to i.e., an escape and refuge.
Derivative: نَصِيرًا (Naseer) means “Helper” and implies someone who constantly comes to someone’s help at the time when it is needed.

Derivative: وَجْهَ (Wajh) literally means “face” and is also used to mean “direction”. وَجْهَ (Wajh) is used to refer to the person as a whole in the case where it represents the most prominent feature of a person.
Derivative: خَلِيلًا (Khaleel) – خُلَّة (Khullah) refers to a close intimate friendship and خَلِيل (Khaleel) is the close intimate friend.

Derivative: حَنِيفًا comes from الحَنَف (Al-Hanaf) which means the inclination away from misguidance towards straightness and uprightness. حَنِيفًا refers to singular focus and dedication and not being distracted from it.

Derivative: الْقِسْطِ (Al-Qist) comes from قَسَطَ (Qasata) which is one of the kinds of words in Arabic that holds opposite meanings: “Justice” and “Injustice”. قِسْطِ (Qist) refers to trying one’s best not to be unjust and to make sure that you are being just. Dealing with others with قِسْطِ (Qist) therefore does not just mean to be just with them but also implies to do whatever you can to not deal with them in unjust or unfair way.
Derivative: الشُّحَّ (Ash-Shuhh) means the combination of greed and miserliness.
Derivative: وَصَّيْنَا comes from وّصَّى – يُوَصِّي – تَوْصِيَة means to recommend and urge, to charge and commission (i.e., a responsibility to undertake), to make a will and also to instruct.
Derivative: حَمِيدًا (Hameed) means that the quality of deserving praise is constantly His and not being in need of the praise of others to be praised.

Derivative: قَوَّامِينَ – قَوَّام (Qawwaam) is the hyperbolised (المُبَالَغَة - Al-Mubaalaghah) form of قَائِم (Qaa’im) which means “standing”. قَوَّام (Qawwaam) therefore implies to be standing firmly and strongly.
Derivative: تَلْوُوا is from لَوَى – يَلْوِي which means to twist or distort and is used in respect to speech and twisting one’s words like when someone gives false testimony.
Derivative: تَعْدِلُوا is from عَدَلَ which can mean “to be just” and can also mean to “swerve and deviate” which in this context it would mean to “swerve” away from the truth and justice.
Derivative: يَعْصِ comes from عَصَى – يَعْصِي – عِصْيان (مَعْصِيَة) which means “to disobey”,

وَمَن يَعْصِ اللَّهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ نَارًا خَالِدًا فِيهَا

- This Aayah addresses the consequences of the one who disobeys Allah His Messenger and transgresses His limits. It follows the Aayah which addresses the one who obeys Allah and His Messenger. An insight when comparing these verses is that the plural of خَالِدِينَ “They will remain forever” is used in relation to the one who obeys Allah and His Messenger whilst here the singular خَالِدًا is used for the one who disobeys Allah and His Messenger. This highlights that the one who enters hellfire will be left alone and isolated whilst the one who enters the gardens of paradise will be in the company of others.
Derivative: يَتَرَبَّصُونَ comes from تَرَبَّصَ which means “to lie in wait”. It is used when you are waiting for an opening or opportunity to strike or gain an advantage.

Derivative: سْتَحْوِذْ is from اِسْتَحْوَذَ which is used to describe when an animal pounces upon another, overpowering it and seizes hold of it so that it can’t break free. It implies the meaning of gaining the upper hand or advantage over somebody or being in the dominant position.

Derivative: مُّذَبْذَبِينَ is from ذَبْذَبَ which is used to depict the motion of something that swings back and forth like a pendulum. It is used to refer those who fluctuate in their position, moving from one position to another or wavering between positions.
Derivative: سُلْطَانًا (Sultaan) means “an authority” and is also used to refer to “a proof” which is authoritative.
Derivative: أَوْلِيَاءَ (Awliyaa’) is the plural of وَلِيٌّ (Waliy) is used in Arabic to refer to a friend who is protective and supportive of you.

Derivative: الدَّرْكِ (Ad-Dark) comes from دَرَك (Darak) which linguistically refers to steps or levels that descend in contrast to دَرَج (Daraj) which refers to steps or levels that ascend. الدَّرْكِ refers to the level that has descended. الدَّرْكِ الْأَسْفَلِ means the lowest level or the lowest depths.
Derivative: أَجْرًا (Ajr) means reward and it is the reward somebody attains for labour, work and effort that he has undertaken.
Derivative: أَمْسِكُو is the command form of أَمْسَكَ which means to hold back or onto something that you already have in your possession.
Derivative: الْفَاحِشَةَ (Al-Fahishah) linguistically refers to an ugly, shameless and hideous act or speech. Here it is referring to the act of Zinaa (adultery).

Derivative: مَا – The مَا attached to بِ in بِمَا is known as ما زَائِدَة (Maa Zaa’idah) which means a ما that is added. It has no function apart from to add emphasis and does not carry the implication of negation or questioning just as it has no grammatical impact as if it was not there.

فَبِمَا نَقْضِهِم مِّيثَاقَهُمْ وَكُفْرِهِم بِآيَاتِ اللَّهِ وَقَتْلِهِمُ الْأَنبِيَاءَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَقَوْلِهِمْ قُلُوبُنَا غُلْفٌ

“Due to their breaking of their covenant and their disbelief in the Aayaat of Allah and their killing of the Prophets without right and their statement ‘Our hearts are wrapped (with coverings)’ …” – This statement is left open without mentioning what it is that will happen to them for all of these crimes. It is said that omitted but implied is a meaning such as “We cursed them” due to these crimes. It can also be argued that it has been omitted to instil fear and leave it to their imagination in terms of what the recompense for all these great crimes will be for them.
Derivative: بُهْتَانًا refers to a serious false accusation, a great slander and calumny i.e., the making of false and defamatory statements about someone to damage their reputation and honour.

Derivative: شُبِّهَ (Shubbiha) is the passive form from شَبَّهَ (Shabbaha) which means “to make similar or to resemble”. شُبِّهَ (Shubbiha) means “Made to appear like or resemble”. شَبَّهَ (Shabbaha) can also mean to obscure something or make it unclear in which case it can imply that “it was made confusing to them”.

Derivative: الْمَسِيحَ (Al-Maseeh) implies somebody that has very distinct qualities which stand out which comes from the use of مَسُحَ (Masuha) in Arabic which implies to be characterised by something. It can also imply that he has been erased and removed of all blameworthy characteristics in the case where مَسَحَ is used to mean to wipe away something or wipe something off i.e., the one who has been purified.
Derivative: حَرَّمْنَا is from حَرَّمَ – يُحَرِّمُ – تَحْرِيمًا carries two meanings. One is to make something legally unlawful and prohibited and the other is to deprive somebody of something.
Derivative: أُحِلَّتْ from أَحَلَّ – يُحِلُّ – إِحْلال doesn’t just mean “to make permissible and lawful” but can also imply to make accessible.
Derivative: صَدِّ is from صَدَّ – يَصُدُّ – صَدًّا which means to obstruct and block and combines the meaning of to obstruct oneself and to obstruct others.

Derivative: الْمُقِيمِينَ (Al-Muqeemeena) – Instead of مُقِيمُونَ (Muqeemoona) which is in the مَرْفُوع (Marfoo’) form and expected here, الْمُقِيمِينَ (Al-Muqeemeena) in the مَنْصُوب (Mansoob) form is used which is a style employed in Arabic to highlight the importance of the word and is also done for the purpose of implying praise (المَدْح - Al-Madh) upon the act. الْمُقِيمِينَ (Al-Muqeemeena) also implies “those who constantly establish the prayer” in the case where the use of the noun (اِسْم - Ism) implies permanency in contrast to the verb (فِعْل – Fi’l) in Arabic.

Derivative: الْمُؤْتُونَ implies the meaning of “those who are constantly giving” in the case where the use of the noun (اِسْم - Ism) implies permanency in contrast to the verb (فِعْل – Fi’l) in Arabic.

Derivative: تَغْلُوا is from غَلَا – يَغْلُو – غُلُوًّا which means to be excessive, to go too far and overboard in respect to something. The meaning here is to exceed and go beyond the proper bounds as a result of getting carried away and going too far.
Derivative: يَحْشُر is from حَشَرَ which means “to gather”. It is the word used in Arabic for herding cattle to a place and as such implies a gathering that is compelled and forced.

Derivative: يَسْتَنكِفَ is from اِسْتَنْكَفَ – اِسْتِنْكَاف which means to believe something is beneath you, where you have disdain for something and are too proud to reject something it. لَّن يَسْتَنكِفَ implies the meaning: “He would never reject out of pride” or “He would never consider it beneath him”.
Derivative: بُرْهَانٌ (Burhaan) refers to a clear and decisive proof that cannot be argued against.

Derivative: رَحْمَةٍ (Rahmah) is related to the word رَحْم (Rahm) which refers to the “womb” of the mother. A situation of being in رَحْمَةٍ (Rahmah) is one in which all of your needs and comforts are taken care of just like the womb of the mother takes care of all the needs of the child. Rahmah incorporates the meanings of love, caring and tenderness.

Derivative: عَاشِرُو is from عَاشَرَ means to live closely with other in close companionship. عَشِيرَة (‘Asheerah) which means a clan comes from this word because it includes close relatives who live closely together.

Derivative: تَعْضُلُو is from عَضِلَ which means to hold back and prevent. It is used in the context of marriage when a husband who no longer wants his wife does not divorce her but rather holds on to her preventing her from moving on with her life and getting married again. Here it implies the husband treating his wife badly and constricting her to pressure her to give up what he had previously given her (i.e., in terms of Mahr) as a ransom for her to escape his hold.

Derivative: حُوبًا (Hoob) has been said to refer to anything that is forbidden and its perpetration deserves censure and rebuke. It has been stated to mean an إِثْم (Ithm) or ذَنْب (Dhanb) i.e., a sin.

Derivative: أَفْضَىٰ – Its origin is فَضَاء (Fadaa’) which refers to expanse and space. أَفْضَى is used to mean that something or someone has reached or arrived to something or someone and is used here to refer to the intimacy between a husband and wife.
Derivative: غَلِيظًا (Ghaleezh) means to be powerful and firm. It expresses the serious of an agreement or contract.
Derivative: مِّيثَاقًا is a contract, agreement or covenant where each party is fully aware of its conditions and stipulations.
Derivative: مَقْتًا (Maqt) refers to something that is detestable and hateful.
Derivative: فَاحِشَةً refers to something that is shameless, hideous and ugly in terms of an act or speech.

Derivative: رَبَائِبُ (Rabaa’ib) is the plural of رَبِيبَة (Rabeebah) which refers to the daughter of your wife from a previous marriage. She is named as such because she is under your care and guardianship i.e., Tarbiyah which is from the same root.

Derivative: حَلَائِلُ (Halaa’il) is the plural of حَلِيلَة (Haleelah) which refers to the wife of your son. حَلِيل (Haleel) refers to the husband of your daughter. They are named as such because they are Halaal for each other.
Derivative: مُّحْصِنِينَ – The one who wants to fortify himself by being chaste, confine intimate relations to marriage and not engage in unlawful relationships outside of marriage
Derivative: الْمُحْصَنَاتُ linguistically means “to be fortified” or “to be made inaccessible”. It is used to refer to a woman who is married or chaste. The former meaning is meant here.
Derivative: مُسَافِحِينَ – مُسَافِح (Musaafih) is the one who engages in unlawful intimate relationships (Zinaa) outside of the remits of marriage.
Derivative: طَوْلًا Refers to the means and capacity in terms of ability and wealth (to get married).
Derivative: تَمِيلُوا is from مَالَ – يَمِيلُ – مَيْلًا and its meaning here is to diverge and deviate i.e., move away from your positions, rulings, principles etc.
Derivative: تَرَاضٍ (Taraadin) is when both parties involved in an exchange have رِضَاء (Ridaa’) pleasure, contentment and satisfaction. It implies mutual consent.

Derivative: تَعُولُوا comes from عَالَ – يَعُولُ and means to deviate or swerve off from the right course. It is used to mean “to oppress” or “to deal injustice to somebody or transgress against them”.

Derivative: قَوَّامُونَ – قَامَ عَلَى (Qaama ‘Alaa) means to stand over something and implies the authority and responsibility over something in terms of taking care of it, preserving it, spending on it, maintaining it and keeping it in order or disciplining it. قَوَّامُونَ (Qawwaamoon) are those who embody the above.

Derivative: نُشُوز comes from نَشَزَ which means to be elevated or to rise (up or high) and can imply arrogance and impertinence i.e., raising yourself higher than your rightful place. It is used to refer to the disobedience of the wife to her husband, her recalcitrance and rebelliousness.
Derivative: فَخُورًا is in the hyperbolised word pattern form of فَعُول (Fa’ool) and means someone who has an excessive amount of pride and is boastful.

Derivative: مُخْتَالًا (Mukhtaal) is the Ism ul-Maf’ool (Active participle – “Doer” form) from اِخْتال – يَخْتَالُ – اِخْتِيَال and refers to someone who is conceited and thinks of himself in high and loft terms which he has imagined i.e., someone who is full of himself.

- It literally means “What was upon/against them” and this construct of مَاذَا عَلَى was used as an expression in Arabic with a meaning like: “What harm would it have been for them i.e., to have done such and such a thing”.

Derivative: صَدُقَاتِ is the plural of صَدُقَة (Saduqah) which in origin comes from صِدْق (Sidq) which means “truth or truthfulness” and it refers to the مَهْر (Mahr) which is “dowry” and acts as the confirmation of the validity of the marriage i.e., its صِدْق (Sidq).

Derivative: مَّرِيئًا (Maree’an) comes from the word مَرِئ (Mari’) which refers to the passage that runs from your throat to the stomach i.e., oesophagus or gullet. It is used with food to mean to go down easily. مَّرِيئًا (Maree’an) therefore implies here to accept what is given to you in ease and do not make an issue of it.

Derivative: هَنِيئًا (Hanee’an) comes from هَنَأَ – هَنْئًا (Han’an) and it refers to something that comes your way without effort or difficulty. It is used with food to describe that food that goes down easily and in a satisfying manner. هَنِيئًا (Hanee’an) therefore implies here that you should accept what is given to you without feeling any discomfort or reluctance i.e., you should take it graciously and with a free conscience.
Derivative: نِحْلَةً (Nahlah) refers to a gift that is given happily and in good will without wanting anything in return.
- مِن لَّدُنْ (Min Ladun) which implies the meaning of “especially from”.
Derivative: هَٰؤُلَاءِ (Haa’ulaa’i) refers to those who are close with the meaning of “these” in contrast to those who are far away where أُولَٰئِكَ (Ulaa’ika) with the meaning of “those” is used.

Derivative: تُسَوَّىٰ is the passive form from سَوَّى – يُسَوِّي – تِسْوِيَة which means to level and even something out. Here in the context of the earth or ground levelling them, it implies the meaning of being at one with the earth and just being dirt i.e., that this would be their end and that they won’t be resurrected.
Derivative: نَصِيرًا is the One who provides نَصْر (Nasr) which is help that comes in difficult or trying circumstances.
Derivative: يُحَرِّفُونَ comes from حَرَّفَ – يُحَرِّفُ – تَحْرِيف which means to distort and twist something in the case where the original of something is changed and edited.
Derivative: رَاعِ is the command form of رَاعَى – يُرَاعِي – رِعَايَة / مُراعاة and it means to “take care of” like a shepherd looks after his flock.

- “So (فَ), they will not believe (لَا يُؤْمِنُونَ) except (إِلَّا) a little (or few) (قَلِيلًا)” – This can mean that “they will not believe except a little” in the case where their belief is only a little or small belief. Or it can mean “they will not believe except a few” i.e., from them.
Derivative: مَفْعُولًا (Maf’ool) means “Done” and implies that the decision has already come to pass and is done i.e., no going back.
Derivative: نَّطْمِسَ means “We obliterate or erase faces”. It comes from طَمَسَ which means to remove a feature of something by wiping it out. Here it implies wiping the features from the face.

Derivative: يُزَكُّونَ is from زَكَّى – يُزَكِّي – تَزْكِيَة which means “to purify” and also means “to attest to the purity or uprightness of someone else”. Here it is the second meaning in the case where some consider themselves to be pure and upright.
Derivative: فَتِيلًا (Fateel) refers to the small thread attached to a date pip.

Derivative: السُّفَهَاءَ (Sufahaa’) is the plural of سَفِيه (Safeeh) which in origin refers to someone who is foolish or weak minded. It refers here to the one who is incapable of disposing of his financial affairs responsibly.
Derivative: الْجِبْتِ (Al-Jibt) linguistically refers to anything linked to superstition and magic. It has also been said to refer to every that is worshipped or obeyed besides Allah.

Derivative: الطَّاغُوتِ (At-Taaghoot) refers to the head of misguidance i.e., its main source and chief inviter. comes from the word طَغَى which means to rebel and it is a rare form of مُبَالَغَة (Mubaalaghah – Hyperbole) with a تاء (Taa’) added to its end.
Derivative: نَقِيرًا (Naqeer) refers to a tiny spot on a date pit or pip. It is used as an expression to indicate to something that is utterly worthless or has no significance because it is so small.

Derivative: نَضِجَتْ is from نَضِجَ which means to become ripe and well-cooked i.e., something reaching the completion of its process. Here, in reference to the skin of the people of hellfire, it means when their skin has been burnt thoroughly to its completion.
Derivative: نُصْلِي comes from أَصْلَى – يُصْلِي – إِصْلاء which means here “To make enter”.
Derivative: نِعِمَّا is a combination of نِعْمَ and مَا. It means: “How excellent or wonderful something is!”
Derivative: الْأَمَانَاتِ is the plural of أَمَانَة (Amaanah) which refers to something that has been entrusted and made responsible to fulfil.

“O you who have believed: Obey Allah and obey the Messenger and those who in authority from among you” – Here the verb أَطِيعُوا “Obey” has only been mentioned before Allah and His Messenger ﷺ but not before “and those in authority amongst you”. This indicates firstly that there is a difference between the first two mentions of obedience and the third. It is understood that the first two mentions of obedience are absolute, unconditional and represent the original source of obedience in contrast to the third.
details.arabic_part: يَا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَطِيعُوا اللَّهَ وَأَطِيعُوا الرَّسُولَ وَأُولِي الْأَمْرِ مِنكُمْ

details.text: Derivative: الْمَعْرُوفِ (Al-Ma’roof) literally means “What is known” and is used multiple times to highlight how something should be done or undertaken. It does not stipulate something that is specific but is rather open to take into consideration the time, place and circumstances. As such it implies that which is accepted to be decent and right within given societal norms.
Derivative: آنَسْتُم is from آَنَسَ which means “to perceive” something i.e., when you see and observe something about someone from the outside.
Derivative: بِدَارًا (Bidaar) is from بَادَرَ – يُبَادِرُ – بِدَارًا / مُبَادَرَة and means to do something in haste or quickly. It implies rushing to do something before its time.
Derivative: يَسْتَعْفِفْ is from اِسْتِعْفَاف which means to back away and refrain from.

Derivative: يَزْعُمُونَ comes from زَعَمَ – يَزْعُمُ – زَعْم and refers to an assumption or claim. It can imply that the one making it believes it to be most likely. It also used to refer to a claim that is false and made in pretence or without evidence. Just as it has been said to refer to a claim or assumption that could be true or false.

Derivative: يُسَلِّمُوا is from سَلَّمَ – يُسَلِّمُ – تَسْلِيمًا and can be considered to be the hyperbolised and more powerful form of أَسْلَمَ – يُسْلِمُ – إِسْلامًا which means to submit. يُسَلِّمُوا تَسْلِيمًا (Yusallimoo Tasleem) therefore means that “They totally submit or completely surrender” i.e., to what he ﷺ passed judgement with.
Derivative: شَجَرَ means “to break out” or “arises” in terms of issues, matters and disputes.
Derivative: يُحَكِّمُوا is from حَكَّمَ – يُحَكِّمُ – تَحْكِيم and it means to make someone or something the judge i.e., the one who makes the decision and passes the judgement.
- وَرَبِّكَ – “And by your Rabb (Lord)” – This is the only time that Allah swears an oath by Himself in the Qur’an indicating to the magnitude of the subject matter of the oath.
Derivative: تَثْبِيتًا (Tathbeet) means to make something ثَابِت (Thaabit) “Firmly established”. It means to consolidate or corroborate something.
Derivative: رَفِيقًا (Rafeeq) refers to someone who is your companion in travel, work and life. It implies someone who you are close to, can rely on and are relaxed in their company.
Derivative: حَسُنَ is one of the verbs that can be used for تَعَجُّب (Ta’ajjub – To express wonder) and implies the meaning of “How excellent” is such and such a thing!

Derivative: الصِّدِّيقِينَ is the plural of صِدِّيق (Siddeeq) which is the hyperbolised (مُبَالَغَة - Mubaalaghah) form of صَادِق (Saadiq) and refers to the one who is consistently and extremely truthful committing to the truth regardless of the circumstances. In addition to coming from the word صِدْق (Sidq) which means “truthfulness” it is also related to the word تَصْدِيق (Tasdeeq) which means “to affirm the truth” in the case where صِدِّيق (Siddeeq) refers to the one who “constantly affirms the truth”.
Derivative: ثُبَاتٍ (Thubaat) is the plural of ثُبَة (Thubah) and refers to a small group of horse riders or cavalrymen like a strike force.
Derivative: انفِرُوا comes from نَفَرَ which means to go out to undertake a particular act quickly. In this context it means to “set forth with haste” i.e., against the enemy.
Derivative: الطَّاغُوتِ (At-Taaghout) refers to any entity that causes you to rebel against Allah.
Derivative: فَتِيلًا refers to a small thread fibre found on a date pip or stone.
Derivative: كُفُّوا is the command form from كَفَّ – يَكُفُّ – كَفًّا which means to hold back and refrain from doing something.
- It literally means “Have you not (أَلَمْ) seen (تَرَ)?” and is used to express the meaning of “Have you not looked into it, considered, examined and contemplated?” concerning a matter.
Derivative: يُدْرِك comes from أَدْرَكَ which means to finally reach, attain or overtake something. It implies that stages are passed through until the matter is reached or caught up to.

Derivative: مُّشَيَّدَةٍ (Mushayyadah) is from شَيَّدَ which comes from شَادَ which means to construct and build something high. شَيَّدَ can carry the meaning of to make that construction be built well and can imply the meaning of reinforcing it. مُّشَيَّدَةٍ (Mushayyadah) can therefore combine the meanings of being tall and of being strongly built and reinforced i.e., impenetrable.
Derivative: الْقِسْمَةَ (Al-Qismah) means division and refers here to “the occasion of the division” i.e., where and when the division of inheritance takes place.
Derivative: بَيَّتَ – بَاتَ (Baata) means to spend the night whilst بَيَّتَ (Bayyata) means to spend the night plotting and scheming.

Derivative: بَرَزُوا is from بَرَزَ which means to manifest. When it is followed by “from a place” it carries the meaning of to “come out or leave” from that place, in the sense that they appear outside of it.
Derivative: أَذَاعُوا comes from أَذَاعَ – يُذِيعُ – إِذاعَة which means to broadcast or spread some information or news.

Derivative: يَسْتَنبِطُونَ comes from اِسْتَنْبَطَ was used to describe the process of extracting water from the well and is used more generally to mean to extract something. Here it relates to extracting the correct information to make the correct deductions from it.

Derivative: حَرِّضِ is the command form from حَرَّضَ – يُحَرِّضُ – تَحْرِيض which means to instil enthusiasm and motivation in people to undertake something, to encourage, urge, fire and rile them up.

Derivative: عَسَى (‘Asaa) implies the meaning that something “may” or “perhaps” happen. It can be translated as “It may be that …”. When عَسَى (‘Asaa) is used in means that it is likely to happen and to happen soon.

Derivative: تَنكِيلًا is from نَكَّلَ – يُنَكِّلُ – تَنْكِيل which means to make an example out of someone through punishment. تَنْكِيل (Tankeel) refers to an exemplary punishment that acts as a deterrent for others.

Derivative: مُّقِيتًا comes from أَقَاتَ – يُقِيتُ – إِقَاتَة. It has three meanings attached to it: 1) To have power, 2) To have guardianship and 3) To have the ability to hold something and not let it go.

Derivative: يَشْفَعْ comes from شَفَعَ – يَشْفَعُ which means to act as an intermediary for somebody in a matter or to intercede on their behalf. It can imply interceding by putting in a good word for somebody or interceding in a good cause, or its opposite in terms of interceding by putting in a bad word for somebody or interceding in a bad cause.

Derivative: كِفْلٌ (Kifl) refers to a share or portion of something that is due which could be good or bad. Here it is referring to having a share in terms of sin. It has been argued that كِفْلٌ (Kifl) can imply double the share.

Derivative: حُيِّيتُم is from حَيَّا – يُحَيِّي – تَحِيَّةً which comes in origin from حَيَّ which means to live and حَيَاة (Hayaat) which is “life”. حَيَّا is used to supplicate for someone with life i.e., to make it long, healthy and wholesome and also came to be used to mean “to greet” someone and تَحِيَّةً (Tahiyah) means “a greeting”. Here حُيِّيتُم is in the passive plural form and means “You are greeted”.

Derivative: أَرْكَسَ which means to return the beginning of something to its end and to turn something upon its head i.e., to reverse it. Here its meaning means “to return” to a previous state implying reversal, degeneration or falling back into something.

Derivative: سَدِيدًا comes from سَدَّ – سَدًّا which means to block and plug a gap in something. سَدٌّ can refer to a wall, barricade or dam. From this the expression قَوْلًا سَدِيدًا (Qawlan Sadeedan) was formed which refers to speech that has no cracks, gaps or flaws in it. Speech that is straight to the point, truthful and prevents the possibility of confusion.
Derivative: الْقَاعِدُونَ – القَاعِد is the one who sits and is used to refer to the one that stays behind whilst others go out for battle.

- أُولِي الضَّرَرِ (Uoli d-Darar) refers to those who possess a ضَرَر (Darar) which is some kind of disadvantage or damage. أُولِي الضَّرَرِ is and expression referring to those who have some kind of disability that excuses them from undertaking something which in this case is Al-Jihad in the path of Allah.

Derivative: سَاءَتْ – سَاءَ (Saa’a) is the opposite to حَسُنَ and expresses the meaning of “How bad” something is or “What a bad” something this is. In this Aayah the meaning is “And what a bad (or horrible) final destination it is!”.

Derivative: حِيلَةً (Heelah) refers to a ploy, scheme or stratagem which is employed to get out of something or to accomplish something. It is also used to refer to a means or the capability to accomplish an end.
